{"author": null, "closed_at": null, "collaborating_teams": null, "collaborating_users": null, "created_at": null, "credits": [{"login": "kaueraal", "type": "reporter"}], "credits_detailed": [{"state": "accepted", "type": "reporter", "user": {"avatar_url": "https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2149767?v=4", "events_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/events{/privacy}", "followers_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/followers", "following_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/following{/other_user}", "gists_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/gists{/gist_id}", "gravatar_id": "", "html_url": "https://github.com/kaueraal", "id": 2149767, "login": "kaueraal", "node_id": "MDQ6VXNlcjIxNDk3Njc=", "organizations_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/orgs", "received_events_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/received_events", "repos_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/repos", "site_admin": false, "starred_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/starred{/owner}{/repo}", "subscriptions_url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal/subscriptions", "type": "User", "url": "https://api.github.com/users/kaueraal", "user_view_type": "public"}}], "cve_id": "CVE-2026-55844", "cvss": {"score": 7.5, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N"}, "cvss_severities": {"cvss_v3": {"score": 7.5, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N"}, "cvss_v4": {"score": null, "vector_string": null}}, "cwe_ids": ["CWE-319"], "cwes": [{"cwe_id": "CWE-319", "name": "Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information"}], "description": "## Summary

The iOS companion app 2023.471 ignores the SSID allowlist for internal networks. This leads to the access token to be sent to a third party in other networks, possibly in cleartext.

## Details

The app uses SSID to detect when to use the internal URL, but whenever the app cannot find any other URL to be used, it fallbacks to the internal URL as well, which can expose user's token when connected to a not secure network.

To protect non-technical users the application should by default force a non-empty allowlist. Otherwise, the leak described here occurs to all people who do not configure an allowlist. Note that an escape hatch would be necessary for people connecting to their home network via VPN or similar, which allows them a secure connection from any network.

The vulnerability was observed in the iOS companion app 2023.471.

## PoC

1. Connect your iPhone via Wi-FI to the network your Home Assistant instance runs in.
2. Connect to your Home Assistant server via an internal URL in the iOS companion app, e.g. http://192.168.0.2:8123/.
3. Under Settings > server name > Internal URL configure any SSID which is not your home network containing the Home Assistant server you use.
4. Observe under Settings > server name that the iOS companion app connects to the server even though not connected to the allowed SSID.

## Impact

The steps above show that the iOS companion app connects to a Home Assistant server, even though it shouldn't as the app is not in the right network. Additionally, for most users the connection via the internal URL will be via a HTTP URL. This means:

* The iOS companion app sends the auth token in cleartext in any, possibly not trustworthy, network as long as it can connect to the IP/hostname configured in the URL. Any observer of Wi-FI traffic (e.g. in a café or similar) can see your auth token.
* Assume an attacker hosts a dummy Home Assistant endpoint at the configured URL in a network you connect to. This endpoint can be built to emulate the Home Assistant WebSocket API well enough and to accept any auth token. This would lead to the iOS companion app to send not only the auth token, but all sensor data it accumulated to the attacker controller endpoint.

## Solution

Starting on iOS companion app version 2025.5.0 the app enforces the SSID requirement through a connection security level choice, user can choose between "Most secure" or "Less secure". "Most secure" will always check for the SSID before connect (or fallback) to the internal URL while "Less secure" gives the user the opportunity to ignore this rule and take over the responsibility on that decision, meaning the app will connect (or fallback) to the internal URL.", "ghsa_id": "GHSA-cm5v-547m-qh5h", "html_url": title: iOS Companion App 2023.471 ignores internal SSID allowlist for connections – possible leak of access token and sensor data
